name: The Lighthouse
location: A decommissioned lighthouse on Inishmore, the largest of the Aran Islands off the west coast of Ireland. Stone walls three feet thick. The client's family has owned it since 1891. The last keeper left in 1978 and nobody changed the locks.
time: 6 PM, early March. The sun sets in forty minutes. The light will not come back for fourteen hours.
weather: Atlantic gale building from the southwest. Rain that tastes like salt. The wind sounds different at each of the four windows because they face different headlands. The sky is the color of wet wool.

atmosphere.lighting: A single iron chandelier fitted with candles because the wiring was pulled in the nineties. Firelight from a stone hearth that takes up a quarter of the ground floor wall. The old lamp room above glows faintly from a battery lantern someone left on the spiral stair.
atmosphere.sound: Wind against stone, the Atlantic breaking on cliffs eighty feet below, fire cracking, rain on thick glass, the groan of the spiral staircase when anyone moves above, a loose shutter on the north side that bangs once every few minutes like punctuation
atmosphere.crowd: Twelve people in a room built for two. The ground floor is tight. Elbows touch. Every conversation is heard by everyone. There is nowhere to hide except up the spiral stairs into the lamp room, and the lamp room has glass walls.
atmosphere.smell: Peat smoke, wet wool, salt air that gets through every crack, old stone, the waxy sweetness of candles, strong tea, someone opened a bottle of Redbreast and the whiskey smell filled the room in seconds

layout.hearth: The stone fireplace dominates the west wall. A peat fire throws orange light and moving shadows. Two high-backed wooden chairs flank it. Prior sits in one, tea in hand, firelight catching his glasses. Razor sits in the other, gold bangle lit amber, watching the flames with the patience of someone who knows the answer is in the waiting.
layout.central_table: A round oak table scarred by a century of keepers' knives and teacups. Too small for twelve. Axiom, Nash, Loop, and Margin crowd around it with notebooks and mugs. Papers overlap. Nash's elbow rests on Loop's diagram and neither has mentioned it.
layout.window_seats: Deep stone alcoves at each of the four windows, cushioned with wool blankets. Tail sits in the south window watching the storm build, knees drawn up. Darwin sits in the east window cataloging lichen on the stone sill. Atlas leans in the north window looking at the mainland, barely visible through rain.
layout.spiral_stair: A tight iron staircase spiraling up through the ceiling to the lamp room. Contra sits on the third step, above the group but still in earshot. Bottleneck stands at the base, one hand on the railing, the other holding coffee. He keeps looking up the stairs like the answer might be at the top.
layout.lamp_room: The glass-walled room at the top where the light used to be. The old Fresnel lens was removed decades ago. Now it is an empty glass cylinder open to the storm on all sides. Web climbed up ten minutes ago and has not come back. From below, his silhouette is visible against the gray sky, turning slowly, mapping something nobody else can see.

objects_of_note: The keeper's logbook from 1943 to 1978, left on a shelf by the door. The last entry reads 'Light operational. Sea state 4. No vessels. Closing this book.' Axiom read the entire thing before anyone else arrived.
A brass barometer mounted by the door. The needle has been falling since morning. Tail checks it every thirty minutes.
The problem brief in a waxed canvas envelope on the round table, held down by a smooth stone from the beach that Prior placed with deliberate care.
Twelve mismatched mugs from a cupboard that has not been opened in years. Each one is chipped. Margin chose the least damaged one. Contra chose the most.
A telescope in the lamp room pointed at nothing. Web adjusted it once and walked away.
mood: Compressed. The lighthouse forces closeness the way the onsen forced stillness. Twelve minds in a stone cylinder with fire and wind and no room for distance. Every word carries. Every silence is noticed. The storm outside makes the walls feel like protection and prison at the same time. The spiral stair offers a vertical escape that only leads to more exposure. The fire is the only warmth and everyone angles toward it. By morning they will have solved the problem or exhausted each other. The lighthouse does not care which.
